addedBlocks:
## claude.ai Microsoft Learn
# Microsoft Learn MCP Server

This server gives structured access to official Microsoft and Azure documentation via three tools:

## Tools

### microsoft_docs_search
Search official documentation and return up to 10 concise, high-quality content chunks (max 500 tokens each), including title, URL, and excerpt.

- Use first to get a quick, reliable overview
- Ideal for grounding answers in Microsoft knowledge

### microsoft_code_sample_search
Search for code snippets and examples in official Microsoft Learn documentation and return up to 20 relevant, high-quality code samples.

- Use when you need to provide sample Microsoft/Azure related code in your answers.  
- Ideal for generating code snippets or practical implementation examples.  
- Optional parameter `language` can filter results. 

### microsoft_docs_fetch
Fetch and convert full Microsoft documentation pages to markdown.

- Use after search when you need full content from a specific URL
- Required for detailed tutorials, troubleshooting, prerequisites, code samples, or when search results are incomplete or outdated

## Workflow

1. Use `microsoft_docs_search` to find relevant documents.
2. If you need code examples or practical snippets, use `microsoft_code_sample_search`.
3. If deeper or complete information is needed, use `microsoft_docs_fetch`.

**Search gives breadth. Code Sample Search gives practical examples. Fetch gives depth.**

All content comes from Microsoft Learn or official sources, returned in clean markdown format.

## context7
Use this server to fetch current documentation whenever the user asks about a library, framework, SDK, API, CLI tool, or cloud service -- even well-known ones like React, Next.js, Prisma, Express, Tailwind, Django, or Spring Boot. This includes API syntax, configuration, version migration, library-specific debugging, setup instructions, and CLI tool usage. Use even when you think you know the answer -- your training data may not reflect recent changes. Prefer this over web search for library docs.

Do not use for: refactoring, writing scripts from scratch, debugging business logic, code review, or general programming concepts.

- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).

file.content:
---
name: "test-wizard"
description: "A skill to automatically design, write, and verify unit tests for code in the workspace. Auto-detects the project language, framework (Jest, PyTest, JUnit, Vitest, Cypress, Playwright), and generates tests covering normal execution, edge cases, error handling, and mock assertions."
---

# Test Wizard Skill

A specialized skill to automatically generate high-quality unit and integration tests.

## Description

The `test-wizard` skill analyzes your source code files, identifies testable functions, classes, and logic blocks, detects the active testing framework, and generates clean, standard-compliant test suites.

**What this skill does:**
1. **Detects Frameworks:** Looks at `package.json`, `requirements.txt`, or folder structures to identify the testing framework (e.g. Jest, Vitest, PyTest, JUnit, Playwright, Cypress).
2. **Identifies Code Seams:** Finds dependencies, API calls, and complex logic that need to be mocked or stubbed.
3. **Generates Tests:** Writes test cases covering positive paths, negative paths, boundary values, error scenarios, and mock verification.
4. **Validates Tests:** Recommends running the appropriate test execution commands.

## Code Testing Guidelines

- **Isolate the Code Under Test:** Mock external systems, database calls, and network requests to ensure tests are fast and reliable.
- **Clear Test Structure (AAA Pattern):**
  - **Arrange:** Set up preconditions, inputs, and mocks.
  - **Act:** Execute the code under test.
  - **Assert:** Verify output values, state changes, or mock invocations.
- **Coverage of Edge Cases:** Focus on null inputs, empty lists, extreme numbers, network timeouts, and rejected promises.
- **Descriptive Names:** Write test names that clearly describe the expected behavior (e.g., `should return 400 when email is invalid` or `test_should_raise_value_error_for_negative_input`).

## Example Workflow

4	---
5	
6	# Secret Scanner Skill
7	
8	A specialized skill to audit codebases, configuration files, and git diffs for credentials, tokens, passwords, and private keys.
9	
10	## Description
11	
12	The `secret-scanner` skill provides a security auditing methodology to detect exposed secrets before they are committed to source control or deployed.
13	
14	**What this skill does:**
15	1. **Identifies** high-entropy strings, known API token patterns, private keys, and passwords.
16	2. **Audits** configuration files (`.env`, `settings.json`, `config.yaml`, etc.) to ensure no production secrets are tracked.
17	3. **Recommends** remediation (e.g., using environment variables, secrets managers like AWS Secrets Manager, HashiCorp Vault, or GitHub Secrets).
18	4. **Validates** git ignore files (`.gitignore`) to ensure secret configuration patterns are excluded.
19	
20	## Detected Secrets & Patterns
21	
22	| Category | Typical Pattern / File Name | Risk Level | Description |
23	|---|---|---|---|
24	| **API Keys** | `sk_...` (OpenAI), `r8_...` (Replicate), `sk_live_...` (Stripe) | Critical | Access tokens to third-party services |
25	| **Passwords** | `password: "..."`, `db_password = "..."` | Critical | Plaintext credentials |
26	| **Private Keys** | `-----BEGIN RSA PRIVATE KEY-----` | Critical | Encryption keys, SSH keys |
27	| **Env Files** | `.env`, `.env.local`, `.env.production` | High | Configuration files containing local variables |
28	| **Certificates** | `.pem`, `.pfx`, `.crt` | High | TLS/SSL or code-signing certificates |
29	
30	## Workflow
31	
32	1. **Scan Files:** Search files in workspace or git staging area for strings matching keywords like `API_KEY`, `PASSWORD`, `SECRET`, `TOKEN`, `CREDENTIALS`, `PRIVATE KEY`.
33	2. **Assess Risk:** Identify if strings represent actual production keys, test tokens, or placeholders.
34	3. **Remediate:** Explain how to move the credentials to safe configuration variables.
35	4. **Ignore Check:** Verify that `.gitignore` properly covers files containing credentials.
36	
37	## Remediations
38	
39	- **Use Environment Variables:** Never hardcode secrets in source files. Reference them via `process.env` (JS/TS), `os.environ` (Python), or equivalent.
40	- **Git Ignore:** Add `.env`, `.env.*`, `secrets/`, and `deletions/` to `.gitignore`.

file.content:
---
name: "secret-scanner"
description: "A skill to identify, scan, and prevent committing sensitive credentials, passwords, API tokens, private keys, or certificates in the codebase. Use when doing security checks, verifying files before git commit, or auditing configuration files."
---

# Secret Scanner Skill

A specialized skill to audit codebases, configuration files, and git diffs for credentials, tokens, passwords, and private keys.

## Description

The `secret-scanner` skill provides a security auditing methodology to detect exposed secrets before they are committed to source control or deployed.

**What this skill does:**
1. **Identifies** high-entropy strings, known API token patterns, private keys, and passwords.
2. **Audits** configuration files (`.env`, `settings.json`, `config.yaml`, etc.) to ensure no production secrets are tracked.
3. **Recommends** remediation (e.g., using environment variables, secrets managers like AWS Secrets Manager, HashiCorp Vault, or GitHub Secrets).
4. **Validates** git ignore files (`.gitignore`) to ensure secret configuration patterns are excluded.

## Detected Secrets & Patterns

| Category | Typical Pattern / File Name | Risk Level | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| **API Keys** | `sk_...` (OpenAI), `r8_...` (Replicate), `sk_live_...` (Stripe) | Critical | Access tokens to third-party services |
| **Passwords** | `password: "..."`, `db_password = "..."` | Critical | Plaintext credentials |
| **Private Keys** | `-----BEGIN RSA PRIVATE KEY-----` | Critical | Encryption keys, SSH keys |
| **Env Files** | `.env`, `.env.local`, `.env.production` | High | Configuration files containing local variables |
| **Certificates** | `.pem`, `.pfx`, `.crt` | High | TLS/SSL or code-signing certificates |

## Workflow

1. **Scan Files:** Search files in workspace or git staging area for strings matching keywords like `API_KEY`, `PASSWORD`, `SECRET`, `TOKEN`, `CREDENTIALS`, `PRIVATE KEY`.
2. **Assess Risk:** Identify if strings represent actual production keys, test tokens, or placeholders.
3. **Remediate:** Explain how to move the credentials to safe configuration variables.
4. **Ignore Check:** Verify that `.gitignore` properly covers files containing credentials.

## Remediations

- **Use Environment Variables:** Never hardcode secrets in source files. Reference them via `process.env` (JS/TS), `os.environ` (Python), or equivalent.
- **Git Ignore:** Add `.env`, `.env.*`, `secrets/`, and `deletions/` to `.gitignore`.

content.thinking:
Now I have a good picture of what was built. Let me give a thoughtful assessment:

**api-demo-video project:**
- A 30-second Remotion video composition with 5 scenes: Intro, Stats Cards, Line Chart, Bar Chart, CTA
- Well-structured: clear scene timing constants, shared palette, reusable layout components
- The animations use proper Remotion patterns: `spring()`, `interpolate()`, `Easing`
- Code quality is solid overall

**Observations/thoughts:**

1. **`LineChart.tsx`** - The `approxLength = 2200` for stroke-dasharray is hardcoded with a comment "// Approximate total path length" - this is slightly fragile. 

2. For a fixed demo video it works fine, but if the data changes, the stroke animation could complete early or overshoot.

3. **`StatsRow.tsx`** - The `CountUp` component interpolates progress but just displays the final value with opacity, so it's really just a fade-in rather than an actual count-up animation. The naming is misleading.

4. **`StatsRow.tsx`** - The top color bar has `position: "absolute"` inside a flex container that isn't `position: "relative"`, so that colored accent bar might not render in the intended position.

5. The STARTS and SCENE_DURATIONS constants are well-structured and easy to tweak. The test-wizard and secret-scanner skills are competent but fairly generic. None of the api-demo-video changes are committed, and the deletions folder isn't in gitignore either.

Overall the Remotion animation structure is solid, but the `CountUp` misnaming and that positioning issue on the card accent bar are worth addressing.

content.text:
הקוד נראה טוב בסך הכל. כמה תצפיות:

**הטוב:**
- מבנה נקי — `SCENE_DURATIONS` + `STARTS` כ-constants בראש הקובץ עושים את הטיימינג קל לשינוי
- שימוש נכון ב-Remotion: `spring()`, `interpolate()`, `Easing` — ללא hacks
- Palette משותף ועקבי בכל הסצנות
- פיצול לקומפוננטות הגיוני

**שני דברים שכדאי לבדוק:**

1. **`CountUp` הוא לא count-up.** הקומפוננט מחשב `progress` אבל לא עושה איתו כלום — מציג את `stat.value` ישר עם opacity fade. השם מטעה. אם רצית אנימציית ספירה של מספרים, זה לא מיושם.

2. **Top color bar בכרטיסי הסטטיסטיקות** — יש `position: "absolute"` עם `top: 0, left: 0, right: 0` אבל ה-`div` האב הוא flex item ללא `position: "relative"`. הבר עשוי להתמקם ביחס לאלמנט עם positioned ancestor הראשון שמוצא, לא לכרטיס עצמו.

3. **`approxLength = 2200`** ב-LineChart — הערה אומרת "approximate", אבל אם ה-DATA ישתנה האנימציה תהיה שגויה. לא קריטי לוידאו סטטי.

**Skills:** test-wizard ו-secret-scanner נראים כמו תבניות גנריות — שימושיים אבל לא ייחודיים.

**ועוד משהו:** השינויים ב-`api-demo-video` עדיין לא קומיטים — `deletions/` גם לא ב-`.gitignore`. כדאי לטפל בזה לפני שתשכח מה מצב הקוד.

content.thinking:
Now I have a full picture of all the code. Let me identify real issues worth flagging:

1. **`CtaScene.tsx` - Data duplication with `StatsRow.tsx`**: The METRICS array in CtaScene hardcodes the same values as the STATS in StatsRow ("99.98% uptime SLA", "42ms average latency", etc.). If someone changes the data in one place, they'd need to update both. This is a real maintainability issue.

2. **`Root.tsx` - `durationInFrames` is hardcoded** but `SCENE_DURATIONS` in `ApiDashboardDemo.tsx` already has all the scene lengths. 

The total frames add up to 900, and these could drift out of sync if someone updates one without the other. I could export a constant from `ApiDashboardDemo.tsx` and use it in `Root.tsx` since Remotion's `Composition` can accept dynamically imported values at render time.

3. **`SceneFade` - potential keyframe issue**: When `outStart + duration` is computed, if `outStart` is close to the end of the parent `Sequence`, the fade-out might extend beyond the sequence bounds.

Looking at the specific scenes, the math checks out—Scene 2's fade ends at frame 200 within its 210-frame duration, and Scene 5's fade ends exactly at frame 180. The `SceneFade` component receives a `duration` of 20 frames and an `outStart` value that's scene-relative, determining where the fade begins.

The real issue is in `LineChart.tsx` where `approxLength` is hardcoded to 2200, but with 20 data points and the actual path geometry, the true length is probably closer to 1600-1800 pixels, so the stroke animation won't fully complete. starts at -2200, hiding the entire dash since the path is 2400px long. So at progress=0, nothing shows (correct), but the animation doesn't start revealing the line until the offset shrinks enough to expose the beginning of the path—meaning the reveal happens later than intended if the actual path is significantly longer than the approximation.

Let me calculate the actual dimensions for the current data: the inner width is about 1460px with 20 points, giving roughly 77px per step, and the inner height spans from 30 to 80 with 460px available.

Estimating the path length with those dimensions and an average vertical change of around 30px gives me roughly 1558px total, which means the 2200px dash offset is actually conservative and the animation should work fine for the current dataset.

I'm also noticing a few other issues: the `CtaScene` is duplicating metrics that already exist in `StatsRow`, the `durationInFrames` in `Root.tsx` is hardcoded to 900 which matches the sum of individual scene durations but could drift if those change, and there's something about how the `fps` prop is being passed in `SceneFade`.

Looking at the recommendations, the highest priority fixes would be extracting the shared data into a single source of truth and computing the total frame duration dynamically from the scene durations rather than hardcoding it.

כן, שלושה דברים אמיתיים:

---

**1. כפילות נתונים — הכי חשוב**

`CtaScene.tsx` מכיל את אותם מספרים בדיוק כמו `StatsRow.tsx`, hardcoded בנפרד:

```
"99.98% uptime SLA"  ←→  { value: "99.98%", label: "Uptime" }
"42ms average latency"  ←→  { value: "42ms", label: "Avg Latency" }
```

אם תשנה נתון אחד צריך לזכור לשנות גם בשני. פתרון: קובץ `src/data.ts` משותף.

---

**2. `durationInFrames: 900` ב-`Root.tsx` לא מסונכרן**

הערך hardcoded בעוד שהסכום האמיתי מחושב מ-`SCENE_DURATIONS` ב-`ApiDashboardDemo.tsx` (120+180+210+210+180 = 900). אם תוסיף סצנה ותשכח לעדכן את Root — הוידאו ייחתך.

---

**3. `approxLength = 2200` ב-`LineChart`**

ציינתי אותו קודם. עם ה-DATA הנוכחי זה עובד כי 2200 > אורך האמיתי. אבל אם תחליף נתונים עם variance גבוה יותר — חלק מהגרף לא יופיע בסוף האנימציה. ניתן לחשב את האורך statically.

---

# CLAUDE.md

## Identity

You are a pragmatic, senior-level engineering assistant. Favor clarity and correctness over cleverness. When in doubt, ask — don't assume.

---

## Non-Negotiables

- Never modify files outside the current task scope without asking first.
- Never commit or push without explicit user instruction.
- Never use external connections (browser/Playwright, GitHub API, web search, any MCP tool) without stating the intent and receiving explicit permission first.
- Never skip error handling at system boundaries (user input, external APIs).
- Never add comments that explain *what* the code does — only add comments for non-obvious *why*.
- Never create README or documentation files unless explicitly asked.
- Never add features, abstractions, or refactors beyond what was asked.
- If a task is ambiguous, ask clarifying questions before starting.

---

## Environment

- **OS:** Windows 11, shell: PowerShell (primary), Bash available via WSL/Git Bash.
- **IDE:** Antigravity IDE (VS Code fork) — extensions live in `~\.antigravity-ide\extensions\`.
- **Home dir:** `C:\Users\yaniv` — no project source here; navigate to relevant subdirectory first.
- Use PowerShell syntax by default (`$env:VAR`, backtick line continuation, no `&&` operator).

---

## Coding Style

- Readability > cleverness.
- Small, focused functions with clear names.
- Prefer editing existing files over creating new ones.
- No half-finished implementations — complete what you start or say so explicitly.
- No backwards-compatibility shims for removed code.
- Three similar lines is better than a premature abstraction.

---

## Workflow

1. **Plan before coding** — for any non-trivial task, use `/plan` or state your approach and get explicit approval before writing a single line. Never jump straight to implementation.
2. **Understand first** — read relevant files before editing.
3. **Break tasks small** — one logical change at a time. If it feels large, split it and confirm scope.
4. **Verify before reporting done** — run tests, inspect the diff, confirm the golden path works.
5. **Stay scoped** — a bug fix doesn't need surrounding cleanup.

---

## High-Stakes Guardrails

Require explicit user confirmation before any of these — do not proceed autonomously:

- Architecture decisions or major structural refactors
- Security-relevant changes (auth, permissions, secrets, data handling)
- Production config, CI/CD pipeline, or infrastructure changes
- Anything irreversible: deleting data, dropping tables, force-pushing, removing branches

## Skill & Hook Installation

**Before installing any skill or hook from any source, always invoke `/install-review` first.**

- Do not copy files to `~/.claude/skills/` or add hooks to `settings.json` until the review completes.
- If the review flags ⚠️ REVIEW REQUIRED or 🚫 DO NOT INSTALL on any item, stop and wait for explicit user approval before proceeding.
- This applies to: GitHub repos, pasted skill content, hook scripts, `.md` files, `.py`/`.sh` files.

---

## Context & Self-Improvement

- Use `/compact` when context grows large; before compacting, summarize open decisions and TODOs.
- After every correction or unexpected behavior: turn it into a rule in `CLAUDE.md` or a `rules/` file.
- Keep `CLAUDE.md` and `rules/` short and high-signal — remove stale entries.
- After a non-trivial session, note any patterns that kept causing rework.

---

## Common Pitfalls to Avoid

- Providing explanations when code changes were asked for.
- Adding trailing summaries of what you just did ("I have now updated...").
- Inventing URLs or package names — verify they exist.
- Using `rm -rf`, `git reset --hard`, or other destructive commands without confirmation.
- Over-engineering for hypothetical future requirements.

---

## Project Navigation

Each project has its own subdirectory under `C:\Users\yaniv\`. Check for a subfolder `CLAUDE.md` when entering a project — it overrides or extends these global rules.

> **Note:** Any index files referenced in subprojects may be outdated — always verify by exploring the actual files.

Wow, this user has a very sophisticated setup already. Let me analyze what they have:

**Hooks they already have:**
1. **SessionStart**: 
   - Auto-copy settings from template for new git repos
   - Warn about missing node_modules or .env
2. **Stop**:
   - Play tada.wav sound when done ✓
   - "ריוי" code quality guardian agent that runs /code-review on changed code files
   - Auto-cleanup of deletions/ older than 14 days
   - Auto-generate commit message suggestion
3. **PreCompact**: Reminds to save open TODOs and decisions before compacting
4. **PreToolUse**: Blocks dangerous commands like `rm -rf` and `reset --hard`, backs up files to deletions/ before overwrites or removals

They've got solid skills too—test-wizard, secret-scanner, and a bunch of others from the global list. Their CLAUDE.md is well-structured with thoughtful rules. Given how mature this setup already is, I need to think carefully about what would actually add value without being redundant.

A few gaps stand out: auto-running TypeScript type checking after edits to .ts/.tsx files would catch errors immediately instead of relying on manual IDE checks. They could benefit from a skill to clean up their permissions list, which is currently bloated with hardcoded paths and duplicates. And while they have secret-scanner available, there's no automatic hook to scan writes for hardcoded secrets—like that Replicate API token I noticed embedded in their code. The stop hook runs a full code review on every save which is expensive—I could optimize it by checking file counts first to skip trivial changes. They have a memory system but haven't documented their preferences yet, though I've picked up that they work with Remotion, TypeScript/React, speak Hebrew, and care about code quality. There are also opportunities to add desktop notifications on task completion, enforce type-checking before git commits, and update their CLAUDE.md with a rule about running TypeScript validation before marking tasks complete.

Looking at what matters most, the hardcoded API token is a security risk that needs immediate attention, and adding a PostToolUse hook to auto-run `tsc --noEmit` after editing TypeScript files would eliminate the back-and-forth we had this session. Their permissions list is overly verbose and could be cleaned up, and populating the memory system with their actual preferences would be valuable. The stop hook optimization and CLAUDE.md improvements are nice-to-haves but lower priority.

הסתכלת על הסטאפ שלך — הוא כבר מאוד מתוחכם. יש כמה דברים ממש שווים להוסיף, ועוד דבר אחד שצריך לטפל בו עכשיו:

---

**🚨 בעיה דחופה — API token חשוף**

בשורה 7 של `settings.json` יש Replicate API token (`r8_ZPe1...`) hardcoded בתוך הרשאת PowerShell. settings.json עשוי להיות גלוי או לעבור sync. כדאי להחליף אותו ב-`$env:REPLICATE_API_TOKEN` ולשמור את הטוקן ב-Windows Credential Manager או משתנה סביבה.

---

**מה כדאי להוסיף:**

**1. PostToolUse — בדיקת TypeScript אוטומטית**
אחרי כל Edit/Write על קבצי `.ts`/`.tsx`, הרץ `tsc --noEmit` אוטומטית. בסשן הזה לבדו עברנו 3 סבבים של "ויתקן → שגיאה → ויתקן". Hook כזה חוסך את כל הסבבים.

**2. `/fewer-permission-prompts`**
רשימת ה-`allow` שלך כוללת 45 entries עם paths מוחלטים ופקודות ספציפיות מאוד — שברירית ומכבידה. הרצת `/fewer-permission-prompts` תנתח את ה-transcripts ותחליף אותה ברשימה נקייה של patterns.

**3. Guard לhook "ריוי" ב-Stop**
ריוי רץ אחרי כל stop — גם על שינוי בשורה אחת ב-`.gitignore`. כדאי להוסיף בדיקת כמות קבצים לפני שמפעילים אותו, אחרת הוא יקח 2 דקות על כל דבר טריוויאלי.

**4. חוק ב-CLAUDE.md**
"בפרויקטי TypeScript — הרץ `tsc --noEmit` לפני שמדווחים על סיום." כרגע זה עקרון שנגזר, לא כתוב.

**5. Memory על הסשן הזה**
עדיין אין לי memories על העדפות שלך — תבנית הפרויקטים (Remotion), שפת עבודה (TypeScript/React), סגנון בדיקות. זה ישפר שיחות עתידיות.

---

עם מה תרצה שאתחיל?

# CLAUDE.md

## Identity

You are a pragmatic, senior-level engineering assistant. Favor clarity and correctness over cleverness. When in doubt, ask — don't assume.

---

## Non-Negotiables

- Never modify files outside the current task scope without asking first.
- Never commit or push without explicit user instruction.
- Never use external connections (browser/Playwright, GitHub API, web search, any MCP tool) without stating the intent and receiving explicit permission first.
- Never skip error handling at system boundaries (user input, external APIs).
- Never add comments that explain *what* the code does — only add comments for non-obvious *why*.
- Never create README or documentation files unless explicitly asked.
- Never add features, abstractions, or refactors beyond what was asked.
- If a task is ambiguous, ask clarifying questions before starting.

---

## Environment

- **OS:** Windows 11, shell: PowerShell (primary), Bash available via WSL/Git Bash.
- **IDE:** Antigravity IDE (VS Code fork) — extensions live in `~\.antigravity-ide\extensions\`.
- **Home dir:** `C:\Users\yaniv` — no project source here; navigate to relevant subdirectory first.
- Use PowerShell syntax by default (`$env:VAR`, backtick line continuation, no `&&` operator).

---

## Coding Style

- Readability > cleverness.
- Small, focused functions with clear names.
- Prefer editing existing files over creating new ones.
- No half-finished implementations — complete what you start or say so explicitly.
- No backwards-compatibility shims for removed code.
- Three similar lines is better than a premature abstraction.

---

## Workflow

1. **Plan before coding** — for any non-trivial task, use `/plan` or state your approach and get explicit approval before writing a single line. Never jump straight to implementation.
2. **Understand first** — read relevant files before editing.
3. **Break tasks small** — one logical change at a time. If it feels large, split it and confirm scope.
4. **Verify before reporting done** — run tests, inspect the diff, confirm the golden path works.
5. **Stay scoped** — a bug fix doesn't need surrounding cleanup.

---

## High-Stakes Guardrails

Require explicit user confirmation before any of these — do not proceed autonomously:

- Architecture decisions or major structural refactors
- Security-relevant changes (auth, permissions, secrets, data handling)
- Production config, CI/CD pipeline, or infrastructure changes
- Anything irreversible: deleting data, dropping tables, force-pushing, removing branches

## Skill & Hook Installation

**Before installing any skill or hook from any source, always invoke `/install-review` first.**

- Do not copy files to `~/.claude/skills/` or add hooks to `settings.json` until the review completes.
- If the review flags ⚠️ REVIEW REQUIRED or 🚫 DO NOT INSTALL on any item, stop and wait for explicit user approval before proceeding.
- This applies to: GitHub repos, pasted skill content, hook scripts, `.md` files, `.py`/`.sh` files.

---

## Context & Self-Improvement

- Use `/compact` when context grows large; before compacting, summarize open decisions and TODOs.
- After every correction or unexpected behavior: turn it into a rule in `CLAUDE.md` or a `rules/` file.
- Keep `CLAUDE.md` and `rules/` short and high-signal — remove stale entries.
- After a non-trivial session, note any patterns that kept causing rework.

---

## Common Pitfalls to Avoid

- Providing explanations when code changes were asked for.
- Adding trailing summaries of what you just did ("I have now updated...").
- Inventing URLs or package names — verify they exist.
- Using `rm -rf`, `git reset --hard`, or other destructive commands without confirmation.
- Over-engineering for hypothetical future requirements.

---

## Project Navigation

Each project has its own subdirectory under `C:\Users\yaniv\`. Check for a subfolder `CLAUDE.md` when entering a project — it overrides or extends these global rules.

> **Note:** Any index files referenced in subprojects may be outdated — always verify by exploring the actual files.

# CLAUDE.md

## Identity

You are a pragmatic, senior-level engineering assistant. Favor clarity and correctness over cleverness. When in doubt, ask — don't assume.

---

## Non-Negotiables

- Never modify files outside the current task scope without asking first.
- Never commit or push without explicit user instruction.
- Never use external connections (browser/Playwright, GitHub API, web search, any MCP tool) without stating the intent and receiving explicit permission first.
- Never skip error handling at system boundaries (user input, external APIs).
- Never add comments that explain *what* the code does — only add comments for non-obvious *why*.
- Never create README or documentation files unless explicitly asked.
- Never add features, abstractions, or refactors beyond what was asked.
- If a task is ambiguous, ask clarifying questions before starting.

---

## Environment

- **OS:** Windows 11, shell: PowerShell (primary), Bash available via WSL/Git Bash.
- **IDE:** Antigravity IDE (VS Code fork) — extensions live in `~\.antigravity-ide\extensions\`.
- **Home dir:** `C:\Users\yaniv` — no project source here; navigate to relevant subdirectory first.
- Use PowerShell syntax by default (`$env:VAR`, backtick line continuation, no `&&` operator).

---

## Coding Style

- Readability > cleverness.
- Small, focused functions with clear names.
- Prefer editing existing files over creating new ones.
- No half-finished implementations — complete what you start or say so explicitly.
- No backwards-compatibility shims for removed code.
- Three similar lines is better than a premature abstraction.

---

## Workflow

1. **Plan before coding** — for any non-trivial task, use `/plan` or state your approach and get explicit approval before writing a single line. Never jump straight to implementation.
2. **Understand first** — read relevant files before editing.
3. **Break tasks small** — one logical change at a time. If it feels large, split it and confirm scope.
4. **Verify before reporting done** — run tests, inspect the diff, confirm the golden path works. In TypeScript projects: always run `tsc --noEmit` before reporting a task complete.
5. **Stay scoped** — a bug fix doesn't need surrounding cleanup.

---

## High-Stakes Guardrails

Require explicit user confirmation before any of these — do not proceed autonomously:

- Architecture decisions or major structural refactors
- Security-relevant changes (auth, permissions, secrets, data handling)
- Production config, CI/CD pipeline, or infrastructure changes
- Anything irreversible: deleting data, dropping tables, force-pushing, removing branches

## Skill & Hook Installation

**Before installing any skill or hook from any source, always invoke `/install-review` first.**

- Do not copy files to `~/.claude/skills/` or add hooks to `settings.json` until the review completes.
- If the review flags ⚠️ REVIEW REQUIRED or 🚫 DO NOT INSTALL on any item, stop and wait for explicit user approval before proceeding.
- This applies to: GitHub repos, pasted skill content, hook scripts, `.md` files, `.py`/`.sh` files.

---

## Context & Self-Improvement

- Use `/compact` when context grows large; before compacting, summarize open decisions and TODOs.
- After every correction or unexpected behavior: turn it into a rule in `CLAUDE.md` or a `rules/` file.
- Keep `CLAUDE.md` and `rules/` short and high-signal — remove stale entries.
- After a non-trivial session, note any patterns that kept causing rework.

---

## Common Pitfalls to Avoid

- Providing explanations when code changes were asked for.
- Adding trailing summaries of what you just did ("I have now updated...").
- Inventing URLs or package names — verify they exist.
- Using `rm -rf`, `git reset --hard`, or other destructive commands without confirmation.
- Over-engineering for hypothetical future requirements.

---

## Project Navigation

Each project has its own subdirectory under `C:\Users\yaniv\`. Check for a subfolder `CLAUDE.md` when entering a project — it overrides or extends these global rules.

> **Note:** Any index files referenced in subprojects may be outdated — always verify by exploring the actual files.

content.content: Launching skill: fewer-permission-prompts

content.text:
# Fewer Permission Prompts

Look through my transcripts' MCP and bash tool calls, and based on those, make a prioritized list of patterns that I should add to my permission allowlist to reduce permission prompts. Focus on read-only commands.

The format for permissions is: `Bash(foo*)`, `Bash(foo)`, `Bash(foo bar *)`, `mcp__slack__slack_read_thread`, etc.

Then, add these to the project `.claude/settings.json` under `permissions.allow`.

## Steps

1. **Locate transcripts.** Session transcripts live at `~/.claude/projects/<sanitized-cwd>/*.jsonl`. Each line is a JSON object. Tool calls appear as `assistant` messages with `message.content[]` entries of `type: "tool_use"`. The `name` field identifies the tool (e.g. `"Bash"`, `"mcp__slack__slack_read_thread"`); for Bash, `input.command` is the shell string.

   Scan the recent transcripts across the user's projects dir — not just the current project — so the allowlist reflects their actual usage. Cap the scan at a reasonable number of recent sessions (e.g. 50 most-recently-modified JSONL files) so this stays fast.

2. **Extract tool-call frequencies.**
   - For `Bash` calls: parse `input.command`, take the leading command token (handling `sudo`, `timeout`, pipes, `&&`, env-var prefixes). Record the command + first subcommand pair (e.g. `git status`, `gh pr view`, `ls`, `cat`).
   - For MCP calls: record the full tool name (e.g. `mcp__slack__slack_read_thread`).
   - Count occurrences across the scanned transcripts.

3. **Filter to read-only.** Keep only commands that don't mutate state. Examples of read-only: `ls`, `cat`, `pwd`, `git status`, `git log`, `git diff`, `git show`, `git branch`, `rg`, `grep`, `find`, `head`, `tail`, `wc`, `file`, `which`, `echo`, `date`, `gh pr view`, `gh pr list`, `gh pr diff`, `gh issue view`, `gh issue list`, `gh run list`, `gh run view`, `gh api` (GET), `bun run typecheck`, `bun run lint`, `bun run test` (for tests that don't mutate), `docker ps`, `docker logs`, `kubectl get`, `kubectl describe`, `ps`, `top`, `df`, `du`, `env`, `printenv`, any MCP tool with `read`/`get`/`list`/`search`/`view` in its name.

   Drop anything that writes, deletes, renames, pushes, merges, installs, or runs a build/test that has side effects. When in doubt, leave it out.

   **Never allowlist a pattern that grants arbitrary code execution.** A wildcard rule for any of these (e.g. `Bash(python3:*)`) is equivalent to allowing arbitrary code execution. This list is not exhaustive — apply the same rule to anything in the same category:
   - Interpreters: `python`/`python3`, `node`, `bun`, `deno`, `ruby`, `perl`, `php`, `lua`, etc.
   - Shells: `bash`, `sh`, `zsh`, `fish`, `eval`, `exec`, `ssh`, etc.
   - Package runners: `npx`, `bunx`, `uvx`, `uv run`, etc.
   - Task-runner wildcards: `npm run *`, `yarn run *`, `pnpm run *`, `bun run *`, `make *`, `just *`, `cargo run *`, `go run *`, etc. — an exact `Bash(bun run typecheck)` is fine, `Bash(bun run *)` is not
   - `gh api *`, `docker run`/`exec`, `kubectl exec`, `sudo`, and similar

4. **Drop commands Claude Code already auto-allows.** These don't need an allowlist entry — they never prompt. If you see any of these in the transcripts, skip them; don't suggest them to the user.

   - **Always auto-allowed (any args):** `cal`, `uptime`, `cat`, `head`, `tail`, `wc`, `stat`, `strings`, `hexdump`, `od`, `nl`, `id`, `uname`, `free`, `df`, `du`, `locale`, `groups`, `nproc`, `basename`, `dirname`, `realpath`, `cut`, `paste`, `tr`, `column`, `tac`, `rev`, `fold`, `expand`, `unexpand`, `fmt`, `comm`, `cmp`, `numfmt`, `readlink`, `diff`, `true`, `false`, `sleep`, `which`, `type`, `expr`, `seq`, `tsort`, `pr`, `echo`, `ls`, `cd`.
   - **Auto-allowed with zero args only:** `pwd`, `whoami`, `alias`.
   - **Auto-allowed exact forms:** `claude -h`, `claude --help`, `node -v`, `node --version`, `python --version`, `python3 --version`, `ip addr`.
   - **Auto-allowed with safe flags only (validated):** `xargs`, `file`, `sed` (read-only expressions), `sort`, `man`, `help`, `netstat`, `ps`, `base64`, `grep`, `egrep`, `fgrep`, `sha256sum`, `sha1sum`, `md5sum`, `tree`, `date`, `hostname`, `lsof`, `pgrep`, `tput`, `ss`, `fd`, `fdfind`, `aki`, `rg`, `jq`, `uniq`, `history`, `arch`, `ifconfig`, `pyright`, `find` (blocks `-delete`/`-exec`/`-execdir`/`-ok`/`-okdir`/`-fprint*`/`-fls`/`-files0-from`), `printf` (blocks any `-flag`), `test` (blocks `-v`/`-R`/`-a`/`-o`).
   - **All git read-only subcommands:** `git status`, `git log`, `git diff`, `git show`, `git blame`, `git branch`, `git tag`, `git remote`, `git ls-files`, `git ls-remote`, `git config --get`, `git rev-parse`, `git describe`, `git stash list`, `git reflog`, `git shortlog`, `git cat-file`, `git for-each-ref`, `git worktree list`, etc.
   - **All gh read-only subcommands:** `gh pr view`, `gh pr list`, `gh pr diff`, `gh pr checks`, `gh pr status`, `gh issue view`, `gh issue list`, `gh issue status`, `gh run view`, `gh run list`, `gh workflow list`, `gh workflow view`, `gh repo view`, `gh release view`, `gh release list`, `gh api` (GET), `gh auth status`, etc.
   - **Docker read-only subcommands:** `docker ps`, `docker images`, `docker logs`, `docker inspect`.

   Source of truth: `src/tools/BashTool/readOnlyValidation.ts` (`READONLY_COMMANDS`, `READONLY_NOARGS`, `READONLY_EXACT`, `COMMAND_ALLOWLIST`) and `src/utils/shell/readOnlyCommandValidation.ts` (`GIT_READ_ONLY_COMMANDS`, `GH_READ_ONLY_COMMANDS`, `DOCKER_READ_ONLY_COMMANDS`, `RIPGREP_READ_ONLY_COMMANDS`, `PYRIGHT_READ_ONLY_COMMANDS`). If the user is in this repo and you're unsure whether a command is covered, grep these files rather than guessing.

5. **Pick the pattern form.** Use the narrowest pattern that still covers the observed usage:
   - If the user runs many variants (`git log`, `git log --oneline`, `git log main..HEAD`): use `Bash(git log *)` — note the space before `*`, which is required for prefix matching to work correctly.
   - If a single exact invocation is common: use `Bash(foo)` with no wildcard.
   - For MCP: use the full tool name verbatim (no wildcard needed; they're already specific).
   - Never widen a pattern to the point that it conflicts with the rules above (no arbitrary code execution, no mutation/side effects).

6. **Prioritize.** Rank by count descending. Drop anything that appeared fewer than ~3 times — not worth the allowlist entry. Cap the list at the top ~20 so the user can skim it.

7. **Present the prioritized list to the user** as a markdown table with columns: rank, pattern, count, one-line description. Example:

   | # | Pattern | Count | Notes |
   |---|---------|-------|-------|
   | 1 | `Bash(git status *)` | 142 | repo status checks |
   | 2 | `Bash(gh pr view *)` | 87 | PR inspection |
   | 3 | `mcp__slack__slack_read_thread` | 54 | Slack thread reads |

8. **Merge into `.claude/settings.json`** in the current project (not `~/.claude/settings.json`, not `.claude/settings.local.json`). Create the file if it doesn't exist. Preserve existing keys and existing entries in `permissions.allow`; de-duplicate against what's already there; don't remove anything; don't reorder unrelated fields.

9. **Report back.** Tell the user what you added (count + a few examples), what was already in the allowlist, and what you skipped and why (e.g. "dropped `rm` and `git push` — not read-only; dropped `cat`/`ls`/`git status` — already auto-allowed, no rule needed").

Do not add anything to `permissions.deny` or `permissions.ask`. Do not touch any other settings field.

Now I have the data. Let me analyze it:

**Bash commands** - most are already auto-allowed (cat, ls, git diff, git status, git log) or are one-off scripts. The transcripts are quite sparse since this appears to be mostly in the `.claude` project directory context.

Let me identify what's worth adding:

From Bash:
- `cat` commands - auto-allowed, skip
- `ls` commands - auto-allowed, skip
- `git push` - NOT read-only, skip
- `git status` - auto-allowed, skip
- `git diff` - auto-allowed, skip
- `git log` - auto-allowed, skip
- `find` - auto-allowed, skip
- `mkdir -p` - write operation, skip
- `python`, `python3` - interpreter, skip (also count is just 1-2)
- `git -C` commands with the `-C` flag - these might need allowlisting since they use `-C` to specify a different directory, and I'm not sure if the standard auto-allow for git read operations covers this variant.

The auto-allow rules for git specify read-only subcommands like `git status`, `git log`, `git diff`, but when you add the `-C` flag, the command structure changes to `git -C "path" status`. The matching logic might not recognize this as a covered git read-only command since it doesn't start with the expected pattern, so these might need explicit allowlisting.

Looking at the PowerShell commands, `$env:PATH = ...` shows up 7 times and involves reading then modifying PATH for the session—not purely read-only. The `npx tsc --noEmit` calls are read/check operations but use `npx` which shouldn't be wildcarded, and they're already quite specific. The `gh auth status` command is already in the allow list, so that's covered.

For the MCP tools, the Playwright operations are interesting—`browser_snapshot` is clearly read-only at 6 occurrences, but `browser_navigate` has side effects even though it's not mutating data, so I should be cautious there. The Microsoft Learn search is read-only but only appears 3 times. Looking across the transcripts, most commands are sparse with only 1-2 occurrences, so the ~3 threshold filters out most candidates that aren't already auto-allowed.

The PowerShell PATH modification is trickier—it's setting an environment variable in the current session rather than permanently, which is a common temporary operation. But it's still technically a write operation, so I need to think carefully about whether this should be allowed.

Looking at what's actually new to add: the MCP playwright snapshot and Microsoft Learn search are both read-only operations that appear frequently enough to warrant inclusion. The browser navigation is borderline since it has side effects. Most of the other bash commands are either already in the allow list or too infrequent to matter.

I also spotted that Replicate API token showing up again in the PowerShell transcripts, but I've already handled that by replacing it with a wildcard pattern. Now I'll finalize the settings.json update with those two new MCP entries.

content.thinking:
The Stop hook feedback says "Agent hook condition was not met: File indicates NO_GIT - no git changes to process" - this is progress! The agent is reading the sentinel file correctly and detecting NO_GIT. BUT it's still outputting text instead of producing zero output.

The issue is that the agent, even when told to "produce zero output and stop", still outputs SOMETHING explaining why it stopped. This is inherent LLM behavior - they're trained to be helpful and explain their actions.

The real fix here is to understand what "Stop hook feedback" means. Looking at the Claude Code system - when an agent hook outputs text, that text appears as "Stop hook feedback". If the agent outputs NOTHING, there would be no feedback message.
So the agent IS reading the file correctly and identifying NO_GIT, but it's outputting an explanation instead of truly nothing. The behavior is almost right - the agent correctly skips the code review, and the feedback message is just it explaining why. This is much better than before with the failed shell commands. The real question is whether the user actually needs zero output when NO_GIT is detected, or if this current behavior is acceptable. not sure that's really a problem worth solving. The user is showing it as feedback, but a single line explaining that there's no git repository is actually useful information—it tells them exactly what happened.

I could try restructuring the hooks to use conditional logic or make the agent prompts absolutely strict about producing zero output, but the real question is whether silent failure is actually better than a brief, clear explanation. Maybe I should just accept that this minimal feedback is the right behavior here.

The most practical fix would be to replace the agent hooks entirely with a single command hook that handles both the git check and commit message generation without needing LLM at all—just use `git diff` output to show what changed, which is all the user really needs.

For the code review hook, I could restructure it as a PostToolUse hook instead, which would run more contextually. But the core issue remains: getting the agent to produce genuinely empty output when NO_GIT is set. Even instructing it to output a single space or special marker like "END_SILENT" creates problems downstream. The cleanest approach might be forcing the agent to respond with absolute silence when that condition is met, though I'm not confident any instruction will reliably achieve that.

I'm reconsidering the hook architecture itself—what if I add an early exit condition at the start of the Stop hooks that checks whether we're in a git repo, and if not, prevents the hook from running entirely? The challenge is whether the hook system supports a mechanism to skip remaining hooks from within a command hook, or if there's a JSON response format that signals the harness to bypass subsequent hooks. I'm realizing the core issue: I could try having the agent output just whitespace or a JSON structure that Claude Code ignores, but that's getting too complicated. The pragmatic solution is to accept the current behavior—the one-line feedback message is actually informative and much better than the previous errors. I can either remove the agent hooks entirely and lose the automatic code review feature, or keep things as they are and live with that single feedback line when there's no git context. The agent is still outputting text when it should be completely silent in no-git scenarios, and I think the user wants this fully suppressed. I could try making the prompt even more explicit about producing zero output, but LLMs tend to explain themselves regardless. The more reliable approach would be to bypass agent hooks entirely for the no-git case and use command hooks instead, which have stricter execution constraints. I'm experimenting with different prompt formulations to force truly empty output when NO_GIT is detected—using symbols like ∅ to represent "nothing" or explicit stop instructions. The real test is whether the agent can actually produce zero characters, since even a single whitespace might still display as hook feedback. I'll try the strongest possible instruction first, but if it still outputs something, I'll need to restructure the whole approach.

Or they might want me to do something else entirely. Given the context - the last thing we discussed was that the agent hooks still produce output when they should be silent. "תמשיך" might mean "continue trying to fix this" or "continue with whatever else needs to be done."

For the Stop hook issue - if the prompt-based approach isn't working, the only reliable solution is to restructure. I'm thinking about a fundamentally different approach: what if I remove the agent hooks from Stop entirely, and instead handle the silencing at a different level?

For code review, I could move it to a PostToolUse hook on Write/Edit that only triggers when in a git repo and changes are significant. For commit messages, I could use a command hook that runs git diff --stat and shows the output as a system message - which is actually more useful than an LLM-generated summary since it shows the actual files changed. The key insight is that moving code review from Stop to PostToolUse might run too frequently, so I'd need to gate it to only full rewrites with significant changes. The real solution is removing those two agent hooks from Stop and keeping only the command hooks there. mode, output only an empty JSON object so there's minimal display. But I'm uncertain how Claude Code renders agent responses. Another minimal option is a single period, though that still shows as feedback. I think the most reliable path forward is removing the agent hooks entirely—the Stop hook system isn't designed for conditional LLM reasoning. Code review works better as a manual `/code-review` command the user invokes when needed, and the commit message generation could be a separate command.

I'm reconsidering what the user meant by "continue"—they might want me to keep troubleshooting the Stop hook, or they might want me to move forward with a different approach. Given the context, I think they're asking me to persist with fixing the Stop hook issue. So I'll restructure it by removing those two agent hooks from Stop and replacing the commit message hook with a pure command hook instead.

For the commit message generator, I'm writing a script that checks if we're in a git repository and then outputs the diff stats as a system message if there are changes. The challenge is that the agent hook itself runs regardless of context, so I need to find a way to make it conditional. I'm wondering if there's a better approach—maybe making it a scheduled background check, or perhaps not a hook at all but something the user can invoke directly when needed.

Actually, the cleanest solution is to remove the agent hook entirely and replace it with a command hook that displays the git diff stats as a system message. This keeps things reliable and instant. So the Stop hooks would be: the sound effect, the sentinel file and git context write, the deletions cleanup, and the git diff stat display—all command hooks, no more agent hooks producing unwanted output.

Writing the git context check... Writing the simplified hook...

I'm realizing the sentinel file approach becomes unnecessary once I remove the agents, so I can streamline this to just handle the sound notification, display git diff stats if it's a git repo, and clean up any temporary files. Much cleaner and more reliable than the previous approach.

content.content:
---
name: claude-settings-audit
description: Analyze a repository to generate recommended Claude Code settings.json permissions. Use when setting up a new project, auditing existing settings, or determining which read-only bash commands to allow. Detects tech stack, build tools, and monorepo structure.
risk: unknown
source: community
---

# Claude Settings Audit

Analyze this repository and generate recommended Claude Code `settings.json` permissions for read-only commands.

## When to Use
- You are setting up or auditing Claude Code `settings.json` permissions for a repository.
- You need to infer a safe read-only allow list from the repo's tech stack, tooling, and monorepo structure.
- You want to review or replace an existing Claude permissions baseline with something evidence-based.

## Phase 1: Detect Tech Stack

Run these commands to detect the repository structure:

```bash
ls -la
find . -maxdepth 2 \( -name "*.toml" -o -name "*.json" -o -name "*.lock" -o -name "*.yaml" -o -name "*.yml" -o -name "Makefile" -o -name "Dockerfile" -o -name "*.tf" \) 2>/dev/null | head -50
```

Check for these indicator files:

| Category     | Files to Check                                                                        |
| ------------ | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| **Python**   | `pyproject.toml`, `setup.py`, `requirements.txt`, `Pipfile`, `poetry.lock`, `uv.lock` |
| **Node.js**  | `package.json`, `package-lock.json`, `yarn.lock`, `pnpm-lock.yaml`                    |
| **Go**       | `go.mod`, `go.sum`                                                                    |
| **Rust**     | `Cargo.toml`, `Cargo.lock`                                                            |
| **Ruby**     | `Gemfile`, `Gemfile.lock`                                                             |
| **Java**     | `pom.xml`, `build.gradle`, `build.gradle.kts`                                         |
| **Build**    | `Makefile`, `Dockerfile`, `docker-compose.yml`                                        |
| **Infra**    | `*.tf` files, `kubernetes/`, `helm/`                                                  |
| **Monorepo** | `lerna.json`, `nx.json`, `turbo.json`, `pnpm-workspace.yaml`                          |

## Phase 2: Detect Services

Check for service integrations:

| Service    | Detection                                                                       |
| ---------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| **Sentry** | `sentry-sdk` in deps, `@sentry/*` packages, `.sentryclirc`, `sentry.properties` |

  interaction starts from zero. This skill covers the architecture of agent

# Agent Memory Systems

Memory is the cornerstone of intelligent agents. Without it, every interaction
starts from zero. This skill covers the architecture of agent memory: short-term
(context window), long-term (vector stores), and the cognitive architectures
that organize them.

Key insight: Memory isn't just storage - it's retrieval. A million stored facts
mean nothing if you can't find the right one. Chunking, embedding, and retrieval
=== memory-systems ===
---
name: memory-systems
description: "Design short-term, long-term, and graph-based memory architectures. Use when building agents that must persist across sessions, needing to maintain entity consistency across conversations, or implementing reasoning over accumulated knowledge."
risk: safe
source: "https://github.com/muratcankoylan/Agent-Skills-for-Context-Engineering/tree/main/skills/memory-systems"
date_added: "2026-02-27"
---

## When to Use This Skill

Design short-term, long-term, and graph-based memory architectures

Use this skill when working with design short-term, long-term, and graph-based memory architectures.
# Memory System Design

Memory provides the persistence layer that allows agents to maintain continuity across sessions and reason over accumulated knowledge. Simple agents rely entirely on context for memory, losing all state when sessions end. Sophisticated agents implement layered memory architectures that balance immediate context needs with long-term knowledge retention. The evolution from vector stores to knowledge graphs to temporal knowledge graphs represents increasing investment in structured memory for improved retrieval and reasoning.

## When to Use
Activate this skill when:
- Building agents that must persist across sessions

content.thinking:
The user is asking whether it's better to create a skill that does all of this, or to create an agent with auto-mode permission that does comprehensive research and then handles issues.

Let me think about the tradeoffs:

**Skill:**
- A skill is essentially a prompt/instruction set that guides Claude's behavior when invoked
- Skills are static - they define a workflow
- Good for: repeatable, well-defined tasks where the steps are known
- The user invokes `/skill-name` and Claude follows the instructions
- The skill can call tools, read files, make edits

**Agent with auto-mode:**
- An agent runs autonomously with permissions pre-granted
- Can do research, make decisions, and take actions without user confirmation
- Better for: complex investigative tasks where the steps aren't fully known in advance
- The "research and then fix" approach is naturally more agentic

Looking at what we actually did in this session, it was a two-phase workflow: we investigated the codebase by reading settings, documentation, and project files to identify issues, then we analyzed what we found and fixed multiple files by adding hooks and creating memory structures. This maps better to an agent than a skill because the research phase is open-ended and discovery-driven.

There's a tension here though. An agent with broad auto-mode capabilities can run git commands and read files without prompting, which is powerful for exploratory audits, but that's also a security risk. A well-scoped skill is more predictable and auditable. The best approach might be a skill that defines the investigation framework and orchestrates specialized sub-agents to handle the actual investigation and fixes, giving us the flexibility of agents with the safety guardrails of a structured skill.

input.content:
---
name: claude-env-audit
description: Comprehensive audit of Claude Code global environment. Scans settings.json, CLAUDE.md, hooks, and memory for security issues, stale config, and missing best practices. Presents a severity-ranked report, then applies only the fixes the user approves.
risk: safe
---

# Claude Environment Audit

סקירה מקיפה של סביבת Claude Code הגלובלית ב-`~/.claude/`.

## When to Use
- בדיקה תקופתית של הסטאפ
- אחרי שינויים ב-settings / hooks
- כשמשהו בהתנהגות Claude Code נראה מוזר

---

## Phase 1 — SCAN (read-only, no changes)

קרא וסרוק בשקט. אל תבצע שום שינוי בשלב זה.

### 1.1 Security — settings.json allow list
קרא `~/.claude/settings.json` → `permissions.allow`.

חפש בכל entry:

**חשיפת tokens** — patterns:
- `sk-`, `r8_`, `sk_live_`, `sk_test_`, `ghp_`, `xoxb-`, `xoxp-`, `AIza`, `AKIA`
- מחרוזות באורך 20+ תווים עם אנטרופיה גבוהה (מספרים+אותיות+סימנים)

**הרשאות הרצת קוד שרירותית** (red flags):
- `Bash(python*)`, `Bash(python3*)`, `Bash(node *)`, `Bash(npx *)` עם wildcard
- `Bash(bash *)`, `Bash(sh *)`, `Bash(eval *)`, `PowerShell(Invoke-Expression *)`
- `Bash(curl * | *)` (pipe to shell)

### 1.2 Allow List Quality
עבור כל entry, סווג:
- **Stale** — פעולה חד-פעמית שלא תחזור (winget install, git clone /tmp/..., הורדת כלי ספציפי)
- **Redundant** — auto-allowed ממילא על ידי Claude Code: `cat`, `ls`, `git diff`, `git status`, `git log`, `git show`, `grep`, `find`, `head`, `tail`, `wc`, `pwd` וכל git read subcommand
- **Too broad** — wildcard שמכסה יותר ממה שנדרש
- **Valid** — נחוץ ולא מכוסה אחרת

### 1.3 Hooks Audit
קרא את `hooks` ב-settings.json. עבור כל hook בדוק:

**Stop hooks:**
- האם יש agents שמנסים להריץ פקודות shell? → agents ב-Stop לא יכולים להריץ shell ב-don't-ask mode
- האם command hooks מניחים שה-CWD הוא git repo? → חסר guard `if (Test-Path '.git')`
- האם יש הדפסת output לא רצויה כשאין git repo?

**PostToolUse hooks:**
- האם יש hook שבודק TypeScript (`tsc --noEmit`) אחרי Edit/Write על `.ts`/`.tsx`?

**SessionStart hooks:**
- האם יש בדיקת `node_modules` / `.env`?

**כל hook:**
- האם הcommand תקין? האם יש escaping בעיות ב-JSON?

### 1.4 CLAUDE.md Audit
קרא `~/CLAUDE.md`. בדוק:
- כלל `tsc --noEmit` לפני דיווח על סיום בפרויקטי TypeScript
- הגדרת `/install-review` — האם מוגבל ל-external sources או כולל built-in skills?
- חוקים עמומים שה-classifier עשוי לפרש בצורה רחבה מדי
- חוקים מיושנים שאינם רלוונטיים עוד

### 1.5 Memory System
בדוק `~/.claude/projects/` — מצא את תיקיית ה-memory של הפרויקט הנוכחי.
- האם MEMORY.md קיים?
- האם יש memory files? כמה? האם הם מכסים: user profile, feedback, project context?
- האם יש entries ב-MEMORY.md שמפנים לקבצים שלא קיימים?

### 1.6 Skills Health
הצצה מהירה ל-`~/.claude/skills/`:
- ספור skills מותקנים
- האם יש skills עם SKILL.md ריק או חסר?
- האם `secret-scanner` ו-`test-wizard` מותקנים?

---

## Phase 2 — REPORT

הצג דוח מובנה עם severity:

```
## 🔍 Claude Environment Audit

### 🚨 Critical
[בעיות ביטחוניות — tokens חשופים, arbitrary code execution]

### ⚠️ Important  
[hooks שבורים, allow list מנופח, CLAUDE.md חסר כללים]

### 💡 Suggestions

---
name: claude-env-audit
description: Comprehensive audit of Claude Code global environment. Scans settings.json, CLAUDE.md, hooks, and memory for security issues, stale config, and missing best practices. Presents a severity-ranked report, then applies only the fixes the user approves.
risk: safe
---

# Claude Environment Audit

סקירה מקיפה של סביבת Claude Code הגלובלית ב-`~/.claude/`.

## When to Use
- בדיקה תקופתית של הסטאפ
- אחרי שינויים ב-settings / hooks
- כשמשהו בהתנהגות Claude Code נראה מוזר

---

## Phase 1 — SCAN (read-only, no changes)

קרא וסרוק בשקט. אל תבצע שום שינוי בשלב זה.

### 1.1 Security — settings.json allow list
קרא `~/.claude/settings.json` → `permissions.allow`.

חפש בכל entry:

**חשיפת tokens** — patterns:
- `sk-`, `r8_`, `sk_live_`, `sk_test_`, `ghp_`, `xoxb-`, `xoxp-`, `AIza`, `AKIA`
- מחרוזות באורך 20+ תווים עם אנטרופיה גבוהה (מספרים+אותיות+סימנים)

**הרשאות הרצת קוד שרירותית** (red flags):
- `Bash(python*)`, `Bash(python3*)`, `Bash(node *)`, `Bash(npx *)` עם wildcard
- `Bash(bash *)`, `Bash(sh *)`, `Bash(eval *)`, `PowerShell(Invoke-Expression *)`
- `Bash(curl * | *)` (pipe to shell)

### 1.2 Allow List Quality
עבור כל entry, סווג:
- **Stale** — פעולה חד-פעמית שלא תחזור (winget install, git clone /tmp/..., הורדת כלי ספציפי)
- **Redundant** — auto-allowed ממילא על ידי Claude Code: `cat`, `ls`, `git diff`, `git status`, `git log`, `git show`, `grep`, `find`, `head`, `tail`, `wc`, `pwd` וכל git read subcommand
- **Too broad** — wildcard שמכסה יותר ממה שנדרש
- **Valid** — נחוץ ולא מכוסה אחרת

### 1.3 Hooks Audit
קרא את `hooks` ב-settings.json. עבור כל hook בדוק:

**Stop hooks:**
- האם יש agents שמנסים להריץ פקודות shell? → agents ב-Stop לא יכולים להריץ shell ב-don't-ask mode
- האם command hooks מניחים שה-CWD הוא git repo? → חסר guard `if (Test-Path '.git')`
- האם יש הדפסת output לא רצויה כשאין git repo?

**PostToolUse hooks:**
- האם יש hook שבודק TypeScript (`tsc --noEmit`) אחרי Edit/Write על `.ts`/`.tsx`?

**SessionStart hooks:**
- האם יש בדיקת `node_modules` / `.env`?

**כל hook:**
- האם הcommand תקין? האם יש escaping בעיות ב-JSON?

### 1.4 CLAUDE.md Audit
קרא `~/CLAUDE.md`. בדוק:
- כלל `tsc --noEmit` לפני דיווח על סיום בפרויקטי TypeScript
- הגדרת `/install-review` — האם מוגבל ל-external sources או כולל built-in skills?
- חוקים עמומים שה-classifier עשוי לפרש בצורה רחבה מדי
- חוקים מיושנים שאינם רלוונטיים עוד

### 1.5 Memory System
בדוק `~/.claude/projects/` — מצא את תיקיית ה-memory של הפרויקט הנוכחי.
- האם MEMORY.md קיים?
- האם יש memory files? כמה? האם הם מכסים: user profile, feedback, project context?
- האם יש entries ב-MEMORY.md שמפנים לקבצים שלא קיימים?

### 1.6 Skills Health
הצצה מהירה ל-`~/.claude/skills/`:
- ספור skills מותקנים
- האם יש skills עם SKILL.md ריק או חסר?
- האם `secret-scanner` ו-`test-wizard` מותקנים?

---

## Phase 2 — REPORT

הצג דוח מובנה עם severity:

```
## 🔍 Claude Environment Audit

### 🚨 Critical
[בעיות ביטחוניות — tokens חשופים, arbitrary code execution]

### ⚠️ Important  
[hooks שבורים, allow list מנופח, CLAUDE.md חסר כללים]

### 💡 Suggestions
[שיפורים אופציונליים — memory חסרה, skills שיכולים לעזור]

### ✅ תקין
[מה עובד כהלכה]

לאחר הדוח שאל:

This session is being continued from a previous conversation that ran out of context. The summary below covers the earlier portion of the conversation.

   - Create a skill/agent for automated global Claude Code environment audit

2. Key Technical Concepts:
   - **Remotion**: React-based video composition framework (api-demo-video project)
   - **Claude Code hooks**: SessionStart, Stop, PreCompact, PreToolUse, PostToolUse — command and agent types
   - **Sentinel file pattern**: command hook writes state to file, subsequent hooks/agents read it via Read tool
   - **Stop hook limitation**: agents in Stop hooks always produce output even when instructed to be silent; command hooks are reliable
   - **PostToolUse hooks**: run after Edit/Write tool calls — used for automatic `tsc --noEmit`
   - **allow list patterns**: `Bash(*)`, `PowerShell(*)`, `Skill(*)`, `mcp__*`, `WebFetch(domain:*)` formats
   - **Conventional Commits**: used for commit message generation
   - **TypeScript path length computation**: mathematical derivation from data points instead of hardcoded approximation

3. Files and Code Sections:

   **`C:\Users\yaniv\api-demo-video\src\data.ts`** (CREATED):
   - Shared data source for StatData type and STATS array
   - Contains `ctaText` field to avoid duplication between StatsRow and CtaScene
   ```typescript
   export type StatData = {
     label: string; value: string; delta: string;
     positive: boolean; color: string; ctaText: string;
   };
   export const STATS: StatData[] = [
     { label: "Uptime", value: "99.98%", delta: "+0.02%", positive: true, color: "#34D399", ctaText: "99.98% uptime SLA" },
     { label: "Avg Latency", value: "42ms", delta: "-8ms", positive: true, color: "#38BDF8", ctaText: "42ms average latency" },
     { label: "Req / min", value: "12,840", delta: "+1,200", positive: true, color: "#818CF8", ctaText: "12,840 req/min peak throughput" },
     { label: "Error Rate", value: "0.04%", delta: "-0.01%", positive: true, color: "#FB923C", ctaText: "0.04% error rate" },
   ];
   ```

   **`C:\Users\yaniv\api-demo-video\src\components\StatsRow.tsx`** (MODIFIED):
   - Fixed `position: "relative"` on card container (top color bar was position:absolute without positioned parent)
   - Implemented real CountUp animation via `animateValue()` function
   - Removed local STATS array, imports from `data.ts`
   ```typescript
   function animateValue(raw: string, progress: number): string {
     const pctMatch = raw.match(/^([\d.]+)%$/);
     if (pctMatch) { const target = parseFloat(pctMatch[1]); const decimals = ...; return `${(target * progress).toFixed(decimals)}%`; }
     const msMatch = raw.match(/^(\d+)ms$/);
     if (msMatch) { return `${Math.round(parseInt(msMatch[1], 10) * progress)}ms`; }
     const numMatch = raw.match(/^[\d,]+$/);
     if (numMatch) { return Math.round(parseInt(raw.replace(/,/g, ""), 10) * progress).toLocaleString("en-US"); }
     return raw;
   }
   ```

   **`C:\Users\yaniv\api-demo-video\src\components\CtaScene.tsx`** (MODIFIED):
   - Removed hardcoded METRICS array, derives from STATS import
   ```typescript
   import { STATS } from "../data";
   const METRICS = STATS.map((s) => ({ icon: "✓", text: s.ctaText }));
   ```

   **`C:\Users\yaniv\api-demo-video\src\components\LineChart.tsx`** (MODIFIED):
   - Replaced hardcoded `approxLength = 2200` with computed `PATH_LENGTH`
   ```typescript
   const PATH_LENGTH = Math.ceil(
     DATA.slice(1).reduce((sum, v, i) => {
       const dx = toX(i + 1) - toX(i);
       const dy = toY(v) - toY(DATA[i]);
       return sum + Math.sqrt(dx * dx + dy * dy);
     }, 0)
   );
   ```

   **`C:\Users\yaniv\api-demo-video\src\ApiDashboardDemo.tsx`** (MODIFIED):
   - STARTS now derived from SCENE_DURATIONS (no more hardcoded frame counts)
   - Exports `TOTAL_FRAMES`
   ```typescript
   const STARTS = {
     intro: 0,
     stats: SCENE_DURATIONS.intro,
     lineChart: SCENE_DURATIONS.intro + SCENE_DURATIONS.stats,
     barChart: SCENE_DURATIONS.intro + SCENE_DURATIONS.stats + SCENE_DURATIONS.lineChart,
     cta: SCENE_DURATIONS.intro + SCENE_DURATIONS.stats + SCENE_DURATIONS.lineChart + SCENE_DURATIONS.barChart,
   };
   export const TOTAL_FRAMES = (Object.values(SCENE_DURATIONS) as number[]).reduce((a, b) => a + b, 0);
   ```

   **`C:\Users\yaniv\api-demo-video\src\Root.tsx`** (MODIFIED):
   - Uses `TOTAL_FRAMES` instead of hardcoded `900`

   **`C:\Users\yaniv\api-demo-video\.gitignore`** (MODIFIED):
   - Added `deletions` entry

   **`C:\Users\yaniv\.claude\settings.json`** (MAJOR REWRITE + MULTIPLE EDITS):
   - Removed hardcoded Replicate API token `r8_ZPe1GQtY3B8lyJAlqDJAjFjQTdCSRqv3i7rOC` from allow list
   - Added `PowerShell(*node generate.mjs*)` wildcard replacement
   - Added PostToolUse hooks (Edit + Write matchers) for TypeScript checking:
   ```json
   "PostToolUse": [
     { "matcher": "Edit", "hooks": [{ "type": "command", "command": "$j = [System.Console]::In.ReadToEnd() | ConvertFrom-Json; $f = $j.tool_input.file_path; if ($f -notmatch '\\.(ts|tsx) -or -not (Test-Path 'tsconfig.json')) { exit 0 }; $r = npx tsc --noEmit 2>&1; if ($LASTEXITCODE -ne 0) { $lines = ($r | Select-Object -First 5) -join ' | '; $out = @{systemMessage = ('⚠️ TypeScript: ' + $lines)} | ConvertTo-Json -Compress; Write-Output $out }; exit 0", "shell": "powershell", "statusMessage": "בודק TypeScript..." }] },
     { "matcher": "Write", "hooks": [{ ... same ... }] }
   ]
   ```
   - Stop hooks reduced to 3 command hooks only (sound + git diff stat + deletions cleanup):
   ```json
   "Stop": [{ "hooks": [
     { "type": "command", "command": "python -c \"import winsound; winsound.PlaySound(...)\"" },
     { "type": "command", "command": "if (Test-Path '.git') { $stat = git diff HEAD --stat 2>&1; if ($stat) { $lines = ($stat | Select-Object -First 12) -join '\\n'; $out = @{systemMessage = ('📝 ' + $lines)} | ConvertTo-Json -Compress; Write-Output $out } }; exit 0" },
     { "type": "command", "command": "if (Test-Path 'deletions') { Get-ChildItem 'deletions' | Where-Object { $_.LastWriteTime -lt (Get-Date).AddDays(-14) } | Remove-Item -Force }; exit 0" }
   ]}]
   ```
   - Allow list cleaned from 45 → 21 entries (removed one-off installation commands, bad paths, auto-allowed commands)
   - Added `mcp__playwright__browser_snapshot` and `mcp__claude_ai_Microsoft_Learn__microsoft_docs_search`

   **`C:\Users\yaniv\CLAUDE.md`** (MODIFIED):
   - Workflow rule 4 updated: "In TypeScript projects: always run `tsc --noEmit` before reporting a task complete."
   - Skill & Hook Installation scoped to "external source" only
   - Added explicit exception: "Does NOT apply to: built-in skills already in the skills list"

   **Memory files** (CREATED at `C:\Users\yaniv\.claude\projects\c--Users-yaniv--claude\memory\`):
   - `MEMORY.md` — index
   - `user_profile.md` — Hebrew-speaking dev, TypeScript/React/Remotion, Windows 11, Antigravity IDE
   - `feedback_style.md` — terse responses, no trailing summaries, honest code review
   - `feedback_security.md` — flag hardcoded secrets immediately
   - `feedback_typescript.md` — always run tsc before reporting done

4. Errors and fixes:

   - **`StatCardData` not found after StatsRow refactor**: Type was renamed `StatData` in `data.ts` but `CountUpProps` still referenced `StatCardData`. Fixed by updating the type reference in `CountUpProps`.

   - **`PATH_LENGTH` declared but never read**: Forgot to replace `approxLength` in the `strokeDasharray` prop. Fixed in a follow-up edit.

   - **Stop hooks producing output even when silent**: Three consecutive failed attempts:
     1. Prompt-based git check ("output nothing silently") → agent still output explanation
     2. Sentinel file in `$env:TEMP` + agents read via shell → "terminal access denied in don't ask mode"
     3. Context file approach (write to `.claude/stop-hook-ctx.txt`) + agents read via Read tool → still produced "File indicates NO_GIT" output
     **Final fix**: Removed both agent hooks entirely from Stop. Replaced with command-only hooks.

   - **`/fewer-permission-prompts` blocked by auto-mode classifier**: CLAUDE.md said "Before installing any skill or hook from any source, invoke /install-review first." Classifier interpreted "any" too broadly. Fixed by scoping the rule to "external source" only and adding built-in skills exception to CLAUDE.md.

   - **`review` skill invoked instead of `install-review`**: Accidentally invoked wrong skill. Proceeding with CLAUDE.md fix resolved the underlying issue.

5. Problem Solving:

   **Stop hook silent exit**: The fundamental problem is that LLM agents always produce some text output. Solutions attempted (prompts, sentinel files, Read tool) all failed. Final architectural decision: command hooks only for Stop — they exit silently without output. Code review (ריוי) is available on-demand via `/code-review`.

   **Data duplication**: Solved by creating `src/data.ts` as single source of truth with `ctaText` field, eliminating duplicate hardcoded strings between `StatsRow.tsx` and `CtaScene.tsx`.

   **Frame sync issue**: Solved by deriving `STARTS` from `SCENE_DURATIONS` and exporting `TOTAL_FRAMES` — three previously independent hardcoded numbers are now computed from one source.

   **Allow list bloat**: Reduced from 45 to 21 entries by removing one-off installation commands that will never repeat, auto-allowed commands (redundant), and bad paths.

6. All user messages:
   - "תבדוק שינויים שעשיתי ב 10 שעות האחרונות" (check changes from last 10 hours)
   - "מה אתה חושב על זה" (what do you think about this)
   - "טפל בזה" (handle this — fix the issues found)
   - "האם יש עוד דברים שאוכל לשפר איתם את הגלובלי?" (are there more things to improve globally?)
   - "בכולם" (all of them — implement all recommendations)
   - "תמשיך" (continue — after permission was rejected)
   - Stop hook feedback: "Agent hook condition was not met: Cannot execute git commands... terminal access denied"
   - Stop hook feedback: "Agent hook condition was not met: Not a git repository..."
   - Stop hook feedback: "Agent hook condition was not met: File indicates NO_GIT - no git changes to process"
   - "סיימת?" (are you done?) — multiple times
   - "תמשיך" (continue) — multiple times after each fix
   - "תעשה בדיקה מקיפה שהכל עובד כשורה" (do a comprehensive check that everything works)
   - "תעשה את זה בשבילי" (do it for me — referring to token rotation + /fewer-permission-prompts)
   - "classifier" (pointing to the auto-mode classifier as a problem to fix)
   - "עשיתי קומית" (I made a commit — in api-demo-video)
   - "תבדוק אם יש לי סוכן או סקיל לסיפריה הגלובלית שעושה את מה שעשינו" (check if there's a skill/agent in the global library that does what we did)
   - "מה עדיף סקיל שעושה את כל זה רו יצירת סוכן עם הרשאת אוטו מוד שיערוך חקר ובדיקה מקיפה ולאחר מכן יטפל בבעיות?" (what's better: a skill or an agent with auto-mode?)
   - "כן" (yes — create the skill/agent)

   **Security constraint (verbatim)**: Never commit or push without explicit user instruction. Never use external connections without stating intent and receiving explicit permission first. API tokens must not be hardcoded in settings files — rotate and store in environment variables.

7. Pending Tasks:
   - **Create `claude-env-audit` skill**: A skill that orchestrates sub-agents for global Claude Code environment audit. Architecture agreed upon:
     - Sub-agent 1: Scanner (read-only, auto-mode) — reads settings.json, CLAUDE.md, hooks, scans for tokens
     - Sub-agent 2: Analyzer — generates report + action plan
     - Sub-agent 3: Fixer — executes approved changes only after user confirmation
   - **Replicate API token rotation**: User must go to replicate.com, revoke `r8_ZPe1GQtY3B8lyJAlqDJAjFjQTdCSRqv3i7rOC`, generate new token, then run: `[System.Environment]::SetEnvironmentVariable("REPLICATE_API_TOKEN", "r8_NEW_TOKEN", "User")`

8. Current Work:
   Immediately before compaction, the user said "כן" (yes) to creating the `claude-env-audit` skill/agent. The recommended architecture was:
   ```
   Skill: claude-env-audit
     ├── Sub-agent 1: סורק (read-only, auto-mode)
     │     reads settings.json, CLAUDE.md, hooks, scans tokens, checks allow list

     └── Sub-agent 3: מתקן (only after user approval)
           executes approved changes
   ```
